abstract: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskites, particularly methylammonium lead iodide (MAPbI3), have emerged as revolutionary materials for photovoltaic applications. MAPbI3 with a direct band gap of 1.55 eV adopts the tetragonal perovskite crystal structure at room temperature. The material is typically deposited by spin coating or chemical vapor deposition. Power conversion efficiencies exceeding 25% have been demonstrated in perovskite solar cells.

abstract: Lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO2) remains the dominant cathode material in commercial lithium-ion batteries. LiCoO2 exhibits a layered crystal structure (space group R-3m) with a theoretical specific capacity of 274 mAh/g. The material is typically synthesized by co-precipitation followed by high-temperature solid-state reaction. Despite its widespread use, alternatives like NMC and LFP are being developed for improved thermal stability.

abstract: Bismuth telluride (Bi2Te3) is the state-of-the-art thermoelectric material for near-room-temperature applications. Bi2Te3 exhibits a high Seebeck coefficient combined with low lattice thermal conductivity, resulting in a thermoelectric figure of merit ZT ≈ 1.0. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) has been demonstrated as an effective method for consolidating Bi2Te3-based thermoelectric materials. The material is primarily used in thermoelectric generators for waste heat recovery.

abstract: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most widely studied photocatalyst due to its chemical stability, low cost, and excellent photocatalytic activity. TiO2 in the anatase phase has a band gap of approximately 3.2 eV, limiting its photoactivity to the UV region. Various strategies including doping and heterojunction formation have been explored to extend its absorption into the visible range. TiO2 photocatalysts are commonly synthesized by sol-gel or hydrothermal methods and are used for water splitting and environmental remediation.

abstract: The equiatomic FeCoNiCrMn high-entropy alloy (HEA), also known as the Cantor alloy, exhibits a single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) solid solution structure. The alloy demonstrates an excellent combination of yield strength and ductility, particularly at cryogenic temperatures. Arc melting is the most common fabrication method for HEAs. FeCoNiCrMn shows great potential as a structural material in cryogenic and high-temperature applications due to its remarkable phase stability.
